name: hawkeye

documentation.readme: HawkEye is a simple tool to crawl the filesystem or a directory looking for interesting stuff like SSH Keys, Log Files, Sqlite Database, password files, etc. Hawkeye uses a fast filesystem crawler to look through files recursively and then sends them for analysis in real time and presents the data in both json format and simple console output. The tool is built with a modular approach making it easy to use and easily extensible.
usage_sections: Simple and modular code base making it easy to contribute.
Fast And Powerful Directory crawling module doing real-time analysis
Easily extensible and vast scanner (Thanks to Gitrob)

arguments.name: --target
arguments.description: Target host, IP, MAC, or URL the tool operates on.

arguments.name: --port
arguments.description: Target port (default: service default).

arguments.name: --interface
arguments.description: Network interface to bind to (e.g. wlan0mon, eth0).

arguments.name: --output
arguments.description: Output file path for the report or capture.

arguments.name: --verbose
arguments.description: Increase log verbosity (repeatable for higher levels).

arguments.name: --threads
arguments.description: Number of concurrent workers.

arguments.name: --timeout
arguments.description: Per-operation timeout in seconds.

arguments.name: --rate
arguments.description: Packet / request rate limit (packets/sec).

arguments.name: --wordlist
arguments.description: Path to a wordlist (users, passwords, paths).

arguments.name: --proxy
arguments.description: HTTP or SOCKS proxy URL for outbound requests.

arguments.name: --user-agent
arguments.description: HTTP User-Agent header value.

arguments.name: --cookie
arguments.description: HTTP Cookie header value.

arguments.name: --header
arguments.description: Extra HTTP header in key:value form (repeatable).

arguments.name: --insecure
arguments.description: Skip TLS certificate verification (lab use only).

arguments.name: --json
arguments.description: Emit JSON output instead of human-readable text.

arguments.name: --no-color
arguments.description: Disable ANSI color in output.

arguments.name: --quiet
arguments.description: Suppress non-error output.

arguments.name: --dry-run
arguments.description: Print what would be done without making any changes.

arguments.name: --yes
arguments.description: Skip confirmation prompts (use with care).

arguments.name: --force
arguments.description: Force the action even if a safety check would block it.

arguments.name: --no-banner
arguments.description: Suppress banner / tool-name output.

documentation.how_to_use: Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `go get github.com/Ice3man543/hawkeye`. Further invocations from upstream: `go get -u github.com/Ice3man543/hawkeye`; `./hawkeye -d <directory>`.

trust.warning: Attribution/index entry only. Audit provenance, code, releases and licence before use.

use_cases:
When you need Penetration Testing, use hawkeye because Hawkeye
[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-_red.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
[![Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/badge/g.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `go get github.com/Ice3man543/hawkeye` as documented upstream for hawkeye.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `go get -u github.com/Ice3man543/hawkeye` as documented upstream for hawkeye.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `./hawkeye -d <directory>` as documented upstream for hawkeye.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `./hawkeye -d /home/ice3man` as documented upstream for hawkeye.
When chaining Penetration Testing tools, pair hawkeye with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `hawkeye --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
